elements.sent-2411-001: Hub-filament systems (HFSs) are potential sites of massive star formation (MSF).
elements.sent-2411-002: To understand the role of filaments in MSF and the origin of HFSs, we conducted a multi-scale and multi-wavelength observational investigation of the molecular cloud G321.93--0.01.
elements.sent-2411-003: The {\tco data reveal multiple HFSs, namely, HFS-1, HFS-2, and a candidate HFS (C-HFS).}
elements.sent-2411-004: HFS-1 and HFS-2 exhibit significant mass accretion rates ($\dot{M _{||}$ $> 10^{-3}$ $M_{\odot}$ yr$^{-1}$) to their hubs (i.e., Hub-1 and Hub-2, respectively).}
elements.sent-2411-005: Hub-1 is comparatively massive, having higher $\dot{M _{||}$ than Hub-2, allowing to derive a relationship $\dot{M _{|| \propto M^{\beta _{\rm{hub }$, with $\beta \sim1.28$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Detection of three compact {\htwo regions within Hub-1 using MeerKAT 1.28 GHz radio continuum data and the presence of a clump (ATL-3), which meets Kauffmann \& Pillai's criteria for MSF, confirm the massive star-forming activity in HFS-1.}
elements.sent-2411-007: We find several low-mass ALMA cores (1--9\,{\Msolar ) inside ATL-3.}
elements.sent-2411-008: The presence of a compact {\htwo region at the hub of C-HFS confirms that it is active in MSF.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Therefore, HFS-1 and C-HFS are in relatively evolved stages of MSF, where massive stars have begun ionizing their surroundings.
elements.sent-2411-010: Conversely, despite a high $\dot{M _{||}$, the non-detection of radio continuum emission toward Hub-2 suggests it is in the relatively early stages of MSF.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Analysis of {\tco data reveals that the formation of HFS-1 was likely triggered by the collision of a filamentary cloud about 1 Myr ago.}
elements.sent-2411-012: In contrast, the relative velocities ($\gtrsim 1$ {\kps ) among the filaments of HFS-2 and C-HFS indicate their formation through the merging of filaments.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Since last two decades, hub-filament systems (HFSs) have emerged as the potential sites of massive star formation \citep[MSF; e.g.,][and references therein]{myers_2009,Kumar_2020,Anderson_2021MNRAS,Bhadari_2022ApJ,dewangan_hfs_2017,Dewangan_2024MNRAS .}
elements.sent-2411-014: From the cloud scale, molecular gas and dust get collected through the filaments to the hub, as a result, hub becomes the densest region of the system with a typical H$_2$ column density, $N$(H$_2$) $>$ 10$^{22}$ cm$^{-2}$ \citep[e.g.,][]{trevino19,Wang_2019,dewangan_2018_dec,Dewangan_2023JApA,Seshadri_2024MNRAS .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The higher column density at the hub leads to a high mass accretion rate onto the (proto-)stars and, hence, the formation of massive stars \citep[][]{McKee_2003ApJ,zinnecker07,motte_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: A recent study by \citet{Kumar_2020 found a large number (nearly 3500) of HFSs in the Galactic plane.}
elements.sent-2411-017: They also discovered that all massive stars above 100 {\Msolar form inside the hubs.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Although the potential of HFSs in the context of MSF is well recognized, their origin remains elusive.
elements.sent-2411-019: In this context, ideas such as overlapping or merging of gravitationally unbound flow-driven filaments \citep{Kumar_2020 and collision of molecular clouds \cite[][]{Balfour_2017MNRAS,Maity_2024arXiv are put forward in recent years to explain the origin of HFSs.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The scenario of overlapping filaments effectively explains the origin of the HFS at G083.097+03.270 \citep{Panja_2023ApJ .}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, reconciling the complex morphologies of HFSs observed in studies, such as Monoceros R2 \citep{trevino19 , G45.3+0.1 \citep{Bhadari_2022ApJ , N159E-Papillon \citep{fukui19ex and N159W-South \citep{tokuda19ex with the model of \citet{Kumar_2020 can be challenging.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: According to \citet{Maity_2024arXiv , the formation of HFSs from cloud-cloud collision (CCC) is a combined effect of turbulence, shock compression, magnetic fields, and gravity.}
elements.sent-2411-023: The connection between CCC and the presence of HFSs has already been observed in several Galactic \citep[e.g., SDC13, W31 complex, W33 complex, and AFGL 5180 \& 6366S;][]{Peretto_2014,Wang_2022ApJ,maity_W31,Zhou_2023MNRAS,Maity_2023MNRAS and extragalactic \citep[e.g., N159E-Papillon and N159W-South;][]{fukui19ex,tokuda19ex massive star-forming regions (MSFRs).}}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, each molecular cloud has a distinct gas distribution in both position and velocity space.
elements.sent-2411-025: Therefore, detailed observational studies of new target sites are essential to enhance our understanding of the proposed ideas of HFS formation and, consequently, to obtain comprehensive insights into the subject.
elements.sent-2411-026: The observational investigations are also essential to determine how the mass accretion rate through the filaments affects overall star-forming activity, including MSF.
elements.sent-2411-027: Massive stars are known for their tremendous radiative and mechanical feedback, which greatly influences their parental molecular environment \citep[][]{Stromgren_1939ApJ,Bressert_2012_new,Dale_2014MNRAS,Geen_2015MNRAS .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Therefore, the study of the origin of HFSs and the effect of mass accretion through the filaments on star-forming activity demands comprehensive investigations at the early stages of MSF, which ensures an undisturbed molecular environment.
elements.sent-2411-029: MSF begins inside hot molecular cores \citep[][]{Mayra_1999,Tak_2004,Paron_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Later, the intense ultraviolet radiation from massive stars, beyond the Lyman limit, ionizes the surrounding gas \citep[][]{Panagia_1973 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: The compactness of the ionized ({\htwo ) regions serves as a proxy to detect the early stages of MSF \citep[see][and references therein]{Maity_2023MNRAS .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: This work involves a multi-scale and multi-wavelength observational investigation of the molecular cloud G321.8$-$0.01 (hereafter, G321), which is at a distance ($d$) of about 1.98 kpc \citep{Atlasgal .}

elements.subsection-2411-006: Role of filaments in mass accumulation and star formation in G321
elements.sent-2411-239: G321 hosts two HFSs (i.e., HFS-1 and HFS-2; see Figure~\ref{321_filament_maps ), where the hubs (i.e., Hub-1 and Hub-2) are connected with several parsec-scale filaments.}
elements.sent-2411-240: As mentioned earlier, filaments are thought to channel molecular gas and dust to their hub, making the hub suitable for MSF \citep{myers_2009,Kumar_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-241: In the case of G321, the total mass accretion rate to Hub-1 through the filaments is {\simi}$2.64\times10^{-2}$ $M_{\odot}$ yr$^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-242: For Hub-2, combined mass accretion rate through the filaments is {\simi}$4.17\times10^{-3}$ $M_{\odot}$ yr$^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-243: In both cases, the mass accretion rates are comparable to or higher than those observed in other MSFRs, such as {\simi 1 $\times$ 10$^{-3}$ $M_{\odot}$ yr$^{-1}$ for DR 21 ridge \citep{schneider_2010 , 1--3 $\times$ 10$^{-4}$ $M_{\odot}$ yr$^{-1}$ for Serpens \citep{kirk_2013 , 4--7 $\times$ 10$^{-4}$ $M_{\odot}$ yr$^{-1}$ for Monoceros R2 \citep{trevino19 , {\simi 7.40 $\times$ 10$^{-4}$ $M_{\odot}$ yr$^{-1}$ for G310.142+0.758 \citep{Yang_2023ApJ , {\simi 6.75 $\times$ 10$^{-4}$ $M_{\odot}$ yr$^{-1}$ for G148.24+00.41 \citep{Rawat_2024MNRAS , and {\simi 1.72 $\times$ 10$^{-3}$ $M_{\odot}$ yr$^{-1}$ for the HFS of RCW 117 \citep{Seshadri_2024MNRAS .}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-356: To understand the formation mechanisms of HFSs, mass accretion through the filaments, and MSF, we conducted a multi-scale and multi-wavelength observational investigation of G321.
elements.sent-2411-357: The key findings of this study are summarized below.
elements.sent-2411-358: G321 hosts multiple HFSs: HFS-1, HFS-2, and a C-HFS.
elements.sent-2411-359: HFS-1 and HFS-2 are connected by a filament (i.e., F4), which shows clear signs of fragmentation.
elements.sent-2411-360: This is a unique result, considering that such complex sites are rare in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-361: HFS-1 and HFS-2 exhibit high mass accretion rates (i.e., $\dot{M _{||}$ $> 10^{-3}$ $M_{\odot}$ yr$^{-1}$).}
elements.sent-2411-362: We found $\dot{M _{|| \propto M^{\beta _{\rm {hub }$ with $\beta \sim 1.28$, suggesting the gravitational influence of the hub on mass accretion through filaments.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-363: The study of color excess sources demonstrates a possible connection among the mass of the hub, its mass accretion rate, and its star-forming activity.
elements.sent-2411-364: Considering the presence of three compact {\htwo regions driven by B1V--B3V type stars in Hub-1 and its high $\dot{M _{||}$ values, we may expect a small cluster of O-type stars to form in Hub-1.}}
elements.sent-2411-365: The analysis of ALMA Band-7 continuum data inside Hub-1/ATL-3 revealed several high-mass branches ($M$ {\simi 8--33\,{\Msolar ), which are further fragmented into branches and leaves.}}
elements.sent-2411-366: We have calculated the mass of these leaves/cores to be about 1--9\,{\Msolar , which is in agreement with the thermal Jeans mass of ATL-3 with subsonic/transonic turbulence.}
elements.sent-2411-367: A significant difference in the evolutionary stages of MSF is observed in G321.
elements.sent-2411-368: The absence of {\htwo regions toward Hub-2 indicates that HFS-2 can be in the early stages of Motte's scheme \citep{motte_2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-369: However, the presence of compact {\htwo regions toward Hub-1 and the ATL-2 suggests that they are in the later stages of evolution.}
elements.sent-2411-370: HFS-1 shows a clear signature of collision with a filamentary cloud about 1 Myr ago, suggesting that the CCC may have triggered the formation of HFS-1.
elements.sent-2411-371: In the cases of HFS-2 and C-HFS, their constituent filaments exhibit relative velocities ($\gtrsim$1 {\kps ), indicating possible formation through the merging or overlapping of filaments.}
elements.sent-2411-372: Overall, we have found G321 to be an interesting MSF region, hosting several HFSs.

elements.sent-2411-374: It is important to note that we have not explored the formation mechanisms of the individual filaments in this study, which can be addressed in future work.

elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width= \textwidth]{velocity_gradient_filaments.pdf} \caption{Panels (a)–(g) show the average velocities along the filaments F1--F7 for the circular regions depicted in Figure~\ref{321_filament_maps}c. elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width= \textwidth]{ATLASGAL_154.pdf} \includegraphics[width= 0.5\textwidth]{Kauffmann_limit_154.pdf} \caption{(a) The panel shows {\it astrodendro}-identified hiererchical structures in ATLASGAL 870 {\micro} continuum image. elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width= 0.50\textwidth]{pv-13CO_F5.pdf} \includegraphics[width= 0.50\textwidth]{afc_com_321.pdf} \includegraphics[width= 0.50\textwidth]{bfc_com_321.pdf} \caption{(a) The Galactic longitude-velocity (i.e., ${l}$--$v$) diagram for {\tco} data. The contour is at 10$\sigma$, where 1$\sigma$ {\simi}0.016 K degree. The integration range for the Galactic latitude is [$-$0.41, 0.37]\,degree. The yellow dotted line separates two velocity components at about $-35.25$ {\kps}. The peak velocities for the blue- and red-shifted components are shown with cyan and magenta dotted lines, respectively. (b) The spatial distribution of the blue- and red-shifted components. The blue- and red-shifted components are integrated for the velocity ranges [$-38.25$, $-35.50$] and [$-35.25$, $-27.25$] {\kps}, respectively. The contour levels for the blue- and red-shifted components are at [3$\sigma$, 6$\sigma$, 9$\sigma$] and [6$\sigma$, 15$\sigma$, 30$\sigma$], respectively. The 1$\sigma$ values are 0.3 and 0.5 K {\kps} for integrated intensity maps of the blue- and red-shifted components, respectively. The areas bounded by the yellow lines are considered for calculating the total mass of the blue-shifted component. The position-velocity ({\it PV}) diagrams are extracted along the arrows highlighted in the panel ``b'', which are shown in Figure~\ref{321_PV_diagrams}. (c) Same as panel ``b'', with red-shifted component shifted by about 4.2 pc. The dashed-dotted and solid white rectangles present the initial and final positions of the red-shifted cloud component, respectively.} \label{321_twocolor}
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width= 0.7\textwidth]{moments-13CO_PV.pdf} \includegraphics[width= 0.7\textwidth]{moments-13CO_PV_2.pdf} \caption{Panels (a)--(g) present the {\it PV} diagrams along the arrows A1--A7, as indicated in Figure~\ref{321_twocolor}b.
